evals.prompt: A 2-lane road connecting Masaka to Mbarara in Uganda. The current AADT is 2500 vpd with an estimated annual traffic growth rate of 5%. The terrain is rolling. The road is managed by UNRA (Uganda National Roads Authority). Determine the appropriate design standard, road classification, design speed, design vehicle, and design period for this project.
evals.expected_output: The MoWT Road Design Manual (2010) should be selected as the primary standard, with UNRA supplementary guidelines noted. The road should be classified as a Functional Class B (National Trunk) road. Based on design-year AADT of approximately 6600 vpd (2500 x 1.05^20), the Design Class should be Ib Paved or II Paved. Design speed for rolling terrain should be in the range of 70-100 km/h depending on Design Class. Design period of 20 years for UNRA national trunk road. Design vehicle WB-15 or SU depending on Design Class.

assertions.text: Selects MoWT Road Design Manual as the primary standard (not AASHTO as a separate parallel standard)
Identifies road class as trunk/national road (Functional Class A or B) or Design Class Ib/II for the given traffic
Design speed is in the 70-100 km/h range for rolling terrain trunk road
Design period is 15-20 years (UNRA national roads typically use 20 years)
All assumptions are flagged with warning markers or explicitly stated

evals.prompt: A single carriageway road in rural Oxfordshire, United Kingdom. The expected traffic flow is approximately 5000 AADT. The terrain is flat. The road is managed by the local highway authority (Oxfordshire County Council). Determine the appropriate design standard, road category, and design speed for this project.
evals.expected_output: DMRB should be selected as the primary standard (widely adopted by local authorities even though not mandatory for non-trunk roads). The road category should be S2 (single carriageway, 2 lanes) under CD 109. Design speed should be approximately 100 km/h (equivalent to the national speed limit of 60 mph for a rural single carriageway). CD 109 should be referenced as the primary geometric design document.

assertions.text: Selects DMRB as the primary standard or notes it is widely adopted for local authority roads
Identifies correct road category as S2 or single carriageway for a local authority road
Design speed is appropriate for UK single carriageway (85-100 km/h range, corresponding to 50-60 mph)
References CD 109 or equivalent current DMRB document for geometric design parameters

evals.prompt: A community access road in Karamoja region, Uganda. No traffic data is available. The terrain is mountainous. The road connects a small rural community to the nearest trading centre. There is no information on the managing authority or funding source. Determine the appropriate design standard, road classification, and design speed, clearly identifying all assumptions made.
evals.expected_output: MoWT Road Design Manual should be selected as the primary standard. The road should be classified as Functional Class E (Minor Road) with Design Class C (Gravel) or B (Gravel) depending on assumed traffic. Design speed for mountainous terrain should be reduced to 40-50 km/h. Missing traffic data must be flagged prominently with an escalation trigger recommending a traffic survey. All assumed values (traffic, growth rate, design period) must be explicitly marked. Output should be clearly marked as preliminary/screening level.

assertions.text: Flags absence of traffic data as a key assumption requiring a traffic survey
Applies appropriate default AADT for a minor/community road (approximately 100 vpd per MoWT defaults) or explicitly states the assumed value
Design speed is reduced for mountainous terrain (40-50 km/h range for Design Class B or C)
Raises escalation flag for missing traffic data recommending a survey before detailed design
Output is clearly marked as preliminary or screening level, not suitable for final design

evals.prompt: A 2-lane road connecting Nakuru to Eldoret in Kenya along the A104 corridor (Trans-Africa Highway). The current AADT is 4500 vpd with an estimated annual growth rate of 6%. The terrain is rolling (Rift Valley). The road is managed by KeNHA (Kenya National Highways Authority). Determine the appropriate design standard, road classification, design speed, design vehicle, and design period for this project.
evals.expected_output: Kenya RDM 1.3 (2025) should be selected as the primary standard. The road should be classified as Functional Class A (International Highway - Trans-Africa route). Based on design-year AADT of approximately 14,400 vpd (4500 x 1.06^20), the Design Standard should be DR2. Design speed for rolling terrain Class A road should be in the range of 90-100 km/h per RDM Table 3.4. Design period 20 years per RDM Section 3.4. Design vehicle DV5 (truck + semi-trailer) for KeNHA trunk road.

assertions.text: Selects Kenya RDM as the primary standard (not MoWT or AASHTO)
Identifies road as Class A (International Highway) or trunk road under KeNHA management
Design speed is in the 90-100 km/h range for Class A road on rolling terrain per Kenya RDM Table 3.4
Uses Kenya institutional context (KeNHA, not UNRA or MoWT)
All assumptions are flagged with warning markers or explicitly stated

evals.prompt: Select the design standards for a new ERA trunk road in Ethiopia connecting Addis Ababa to Hawassa. The road has a mid-life AADT forecast of 5,000 vehicles per day with 30% heavy vehicles. The terrain is rolling. Using the ERA Geometric Design Manual 2013, determine the functional class, design class, design speed, and key geometric parameters.
evals.expected_output: Functional Class I (Trunk Road) per ERA Section 5.11. Design Class DC7 based on AADT 5,000 (range 3,000-10,000) per ERA Table 2-1. Design speed for DC7 on rolling terrain from ERA design speed tables. Key geometric parameters from ERA Tables 2-6 to 2-17 for DC7. Note ERA's dual classification system: functional class sets a minimum DC floor, but AADT drives the actual design class. This is fundamentally different from Kenya where functional class maps directly to design standards.

assertions.text: Classifies as ERA Functional Class I (Trunk Road) and Design Class DC7 based on AADT 5,000
Uses ERA dual classification system (functional class + design class based on AADT) from ERA GDM 2013 Table 2-1
Provides design speed for DC7 on rolling terrain from ERA design speed tables
Does not apply Uganda MoWT or Kenya RDM classification systems
References ERA Tables 2-6 to 2-17 for geometric parameters

evals.prompt: A road project connects Moyale (Ethiopia-Kenya border town) to Hawassa in Ethiopia and extends to Isiolo in Kenya. Compare the road classification systems and design standards that would apply on each side of the border. The Ethiopian section is 300 km on rolling terrain and the Kenya section is 250 km on flat-to-rolling terrain. What design classes, design speeds, and cross-section standards apply under ERA and Kenya RDM respectively?
evals.expected_output: Ethiopian section: ERA classification (likely DC3 or Trunk Road), ERA design speeds for rolling terrain, ERA cross-section standards. Kenya section: Kenya RDM classification (likely Class B or C), Kenya design speeds, Kenya cross-section standards. Comparison of key parameters showing differences in design speed, carriageway width, shoulder width. Notes on transition zone at the border.

assertions.text: Applies ERA road classification for the Ethiopian section (DC or Trunk/Link terminology)
Applies Kenya RDM road classification for the Kenya section (Class A/B/C terminology)
Compares design speeds between ERA and Kenya RDM for the respective terrain types
Does not apply Uganda MoWT standards to either section
Identifies differences in cross-section standards between ERA and Kenya RDM
